coredata.dc:title: G-quadruplex-based detection of glyphosate in complex biological systems by a time-resolved luminescent assay
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Feng
Li, Guodong
Liu, Hao
Leung, Chung-Hang
Ma, Dik-Lung
coredata.dc:description: A luminescent G-quadruplex-derived detection technique was established to monitor glyphosate (GLY) using a phosphorescent iridium(III)-based probe. By exploiting the long lifetime of the probe, the use of time-resolved emission spectroscopy (TRES) could overcome substantial fluorescence background interference. This platform displayed excellent sensitivity for GLY with a limit of detection of 26.4 nM in Tris buffer, and 79.3 nM in Tris buffer containing soybean extract to simulate a complex environment. Due to the specificity of the GLY aptamer, the platform could selectively recognize GLY in complex samples. The feasibility of the method was evaluated by analysis of licorice, ginseng, ginger, and soybean samples, as well as in HUVEC and LO2 cell extracts, with good recovery values ranging from 85 % to 110 %. Importantly, GLY excreted by mouse fed with GLY could also be rapidly and efficiently detected with high correlation with a commercial kit, indicating the reliability of this platform for the rapid and efficient monitoring GLY in animals exposed to GLY. Finally, besides being a rapid and efficient detection method, this method also has the potential for rapid diagnosis at the point of care.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-quadruplex
Glyphosate
Time-Resolved emission spectroscopy
Iridium(III) complex probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence response of complex 1 toward various kinds of DNA
Time-resolved measurement of GLY in mouse urine
Optimizing the detection settings for GLY
Detection of GLY in complex environments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme showing the operation of the aptamer-based sensing system for GLY employing a G-quadruplex-selective iridium(III) complex in TRES mode.

(a) Chemical structure of complex 1. (b) Emission intensity of 1 (1 μM) when incubated with 5 μM of various nucleic acids. (c) FRET-melting experiment of F21 T G-quadruplex DNA with 1. (d) FRET-melting experiment of F10 T ssDNA with 1.
(a) TRES of complex 1 or organic dye Cou460 with 0 or 333 ns decay time. (b) TRES of complex 1 with or without GLY with 333 ns decay time.

(a) Relative emission of the system with various concentrations of 1 (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 μM). (b) Relative emission of the system with various concentrations of KCl (50, 100, 150, and 200 mM). (c) Relative emission at different pH values (6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, and 8.5). (d) Relative emission at different denaturation temperatures (75, 85, 90, 95, and 100 °C). (e) Relative emission at different concentrations of aptamer (50, 80, 200, 500, and 1000 nM). (f) Relative emission at different denaturation times (1, 3, 5, 10, and 15 min.). (g) Relative emission at different incubation times (1, 5, 10, 15, and 20 min.). Data are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3), P values were calculated using a Two-sided T-test. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, vs. indicated group.
(a) TRES signal with increasing concentrations of GLY (0.1-500 nM) in Tris buffer. (b) Emission intensity at 560 nm vs. GLY.

(a) Luminescence signal of the system for GLY, methomyl, PDT, prometryne and warfarin in Tris buffer. (b) Emission signal of the system with other interfering ions in tris buffer. Data are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3), P values were calculated using a Two-sided T-test. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs. indicated group.
Detection of GLY in plant and cell extracts. "N.D." means that the concentration of glyphosate is too low to be tested in actual samples using ELISA kit.
Detection of GLY in mouse urine using the TRES method and correlation coefficients to ELISA.

head.ce:title: G-quadruplex-based detection of glyphosate in complex biological systems by a time-resolved luminescent assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A luminescent G-quadruplex-derived detection technique was established to monitor glyphosate (GLY) using a phosphorescent iridium(III)-based probe. By exploiting the long lifetime of the probe, the use of time-resolved emission spectroscopy (TRES) could overcome substantial fluorescence background interference. This platform displayed excellent sensitivity for GLY with a limit of detection of 26.4 nM in Tris buffer, and 79.3 nM in Tris buffer containing soybean extract to simulate a complex environment. Due to the specificity of the GLY aptamer, the platform could selectively recognize GLY in complex samples. The feasibility of the method was evaluated by analysis of licorice, ginseng, ginger, and soybean samples, as well as in HUVEC and LO2 cell extracts, with good recovery values ranging from 85 % to 110 %. Importantly, GLY excreted by mouse fed with GLY could also be rapidly and efficiently detected with high correlation with a commercial kit, indicating the reliability of this platform for the rapid and efficient monitoring GLY in animals exposed to GLY. Finally, besides being a rapid and efficient detection method, this method also has the potential for rapid diagnosis at the point of care.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (GLY) is a herbicide which has been used worldwide since in the 1970s [1]. Its main chemical composition is phosphonate, which can kill annual broadleaf weeds that compete with crops [2]. Although GLY has lower toxicity to mammals compared to other herbicides, it is still a potential hazard to humans [3,4]. GLY exposure may occur through contaminated drinking water and agriculture. GLY can induce cell death through apoptosis and autophagy [5,6]. The maximum contaminant level of GLY in drinking water stipulated by the US Environmental Protection Agency is 0.7 μg/mL (4.14 μM) [7]. Meanwhile, the maximum residual level (MRL) of GLY in most crops in the European Union is set to 0.1 μg/g (0.59 μM) [8]. In China, the MRL of GLY in fruits is 0.5 mg/kg (2.95 μM) [9]. Hence, the detection of GLY in foodstuffs and biological systems is of utmost importance.

Many methods have been used to detect GLY such as gas chromatography, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, high performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and ion chromatography [10–14]. These methods can offer sensitive and accurate results, but require complicated sample preparation and longer analysis times. These methods are only suitable for analysis in the laboratory and cannot be operated in the field for real-time detection. Therefore, it is necessary to invent novel techniques for the selective detection of GLY that are rapid and accurate.

Fluorescence techniques have been used for GLY monitoring [15]. However, typical fluorescence methods using organic dyes are susceptible to interference from ubiquitous endogenous fluorescent components in the environment [16]. Using time-resolved emission spectroscopy (TRES), long-lived phosphorescence can be recorded after the short-lived fluorescent background has decayed. [17–19]. Thus, it is well suited to discriminating between fluorophores with overlapping emission spectra but different decay times [20]. Luminescent transition metal complexes, particularly iridium(III) complexes, have been utilized for TRES owing to their superior optical properties and long-lived phosphorescence [21].

Nucleic acid aptamers are single-stranded oligonucleotides which are isolated from random-sequence DNA pools through in vitro selection process termed Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential Enrichment [22]. Aptamers are capable of binding tightly, selectively, sensitively and specifically to targets ranging in size from small organic molecules, proteins, even to cells [23,24]. A number of aptamer sequences undergo a conformational change into a G-quadruplex motif upon binding to specific small molecules [25,26].The core G-quadruplex unit is the guanine tetrad, which is formed from square-planar arrangements of guanine bases linked by Hoogsteen hydrogen bonding and monovalent cations [27,28]. Due to its fascinating structural diversity, the G-quadruplex structure has found extensive use for the development of analytical assays [29].

Meanwhile, some luminescent iridium(III) metal complexes possess photophysical properties that are strongly affected by subtle changes of their local environment, allowing for the luminescent visualization of G-quadruplex DNA [30]. Hence, the conformational transition of aptamers into G-quadruplex motifs upon binding to their cognate ligands can be effectively monitored by the use of a G-quadruplex-selective luminescent iridium(III) complex, thereby transducing the analyte binding event into a luminescent enhancement response [31,32]. The enhanced luminescence of the complex in the presence of G-quadruplex DNA is due to effective end-stacking interaction of the planar and aromatic moiety of complex at the end-termini of G-quadruplex. This shields the metal centre from solvent interactions and suppresses non-radiative decay of the excited state, thus enhancing its triplet metal-to-ligand charge transfer (3MLCT) emission [33].

G-quadruplex-selective iridium(III) complexes have emerged as versatile probes for sensing applications, including for small molecules, biomolecules, enzymatic activities [27,34,35]. Herein, we developed a sensitive and rapid aptamer-based TRES luminescent platform for sensing GLY, employing the G-quadruplex-selective iridium(III) complex 1 and a reported GLY aptamer [36]. The G-quadruplex structure formed by the aptamer in the presence of GLY has been previously confirmed by MFOLD software, which showed a G-quadruplex secondary structure containing protruding rings and stems [36]. Previous circular dichroism experiments also showed an increasing peak at 275 nm with increasing concentrations of K+, which further supports the formation of the G-quadruplex induced by GLY [36].

The schematic of the 1/G-quadruplex-based GLY sensing system by using TRES is presented (Fig. 1 ). The GLY aptamer adopts a random structure when GLY is absent. The iridium(III) complex 1 probe does not bind effectively to single-stranded DNA, which results in weak emission. In the presence of GLY, the aptamer sequence adopts a G-quadruplex conformation, which is recognized by complex 1 with an enhanced luminescent response [37]. The phosphorescence emission of complex 1 is long-lived, so it can be separated from interfering background fluorescence using TRES. This platform was optimized to achieve excellent sensitivity and selectivity for GLY. Moreover, it was demonstrated to function effectively in various foodstuffs, cell extracts, and mouse urine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence response of complex 1 toward various kinds of DNA
ce:section.ce:para: SsDNA, dsDNA, or G-quadruplex DNAs (Table S1) were annealed by heating at 95 °C for 5 min to denature DNA in Tris buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5), followed by cooling to 4 °C over ten minutes. Complex 1 was added at a final concentration of 1.0 μM, and the luminescence response was measured at 450−690 nm after excitation of the solution at 352 nm using on a Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer (Horiba Instruments).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-resolved measurement of GLY in mouse urine
ce:section.ce:para: Animal studies were sanctioned by the Animal Ethical and Welfare Committee of University of Macau (No. UMARE-035-2019). Male C57BL/6 J mice at 6–8 weeks old were housed in the animal facility of the University of Macau, maintained at 23 ± 11 °C with 12 h light/dark cycles with unrestricted access to food and water. Animals weighing about 20 g were split into two random groups. In the GLY group, mice were fed via intragastric administration with 300 μL water containing 0.5 mg GLY. The control animals were fed with 300 μL water. Urine was collected every 24 h and centrifuged before being placed in a freezer at -20 °C. Before luminescence detection, the solutions were diluted 10-fold with distilled water, then the aptamer (80 nM) and complex 1 (1 μM) were added. The sample was treated at 95 °C for 3 min, and subsequently chilled to 4 °C over 10 min to allow G-quadruplex formation. Then, TRES was used to record the luminescence signal as above. Validation was performed using an ELISA kit for GLY (Abraxis, USA) following to the manufacturer’s instructions [38]. The correlation coefficient between the two assays was calculated.

The iridium(III) complex 1 (Ir(epq)2(4,7-dmphen)) (where epq = 2-(4-ethylphenyl) quinoline and dmphen = 4,7- dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline) (Fig. 2 a) was generated using a modification of a reported method [39]. In acetonitrile, complex 1 displayed an excitation peak at 352 nm and an emission peak at 561 nm (Fig. S1).

The emission of complex 1 in the presence of various kinds of DNA was measured (Table S1). The emission of complex 1 was strongly enhanced when incubated with the G-quadruplexes Pu27 and PS2.M (Fig. 2b). In contrast, there was no obvious variation in luminescence when 1 was incubated with ssDNA or dsDNA. This allows complex 1 to act as a signal transducer when a quadruplex scaffold is generated from a ssDNA or dsDNA substrate. To measure the avidity of complex 1 to G-quadruplexes, we conducted the FRET melting assay using F21 T, a doubly-labeled G-quadruplex DNA sequence with a donor and acceptor pair [40]. Disruption of the quadruplex topology separates the donor and acceptor motifs, which increase the FRET signal [41]. However, ligand binding will strengthen the structure of the G-quadruplex, thus raising the melting temperature (ΔTm) [37,42]. Encouragingly, our results showed that complex 1 (7 μM) increased ΔTm of the F21 T G-quadruplex by 5 °C (Fig. 2c), indicating that the complex can stabilize DNA. In contrast, the stability of F10 T, a single-stranded DNA control sequence [43], was increased by only 1.0 °C (Fig. 2d). Hence, complex 1 is selective for G-quadruplexes compared to other DNA forms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the detection settings for GLY
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the advantages of the TRES technique, the organic dye Coumarin460 (Cou460) was employed as a representative fluorescent interfering substance in soybean extract. Cou460 shows intrinsic fluorescence at 460 nm in steady-state mode (i.e., with a 0 ns decay time), which could interfere with the luminescent signal of complex 1 leading to difficulties in the determination of GLY (Fig. 3 a). On the other and, with a 333 ns decay time which is less than the lifetime of the probe (523 ns) but longer than the lifetime of Cou460, the short-lived fluorescence of Cou460 nearly disappears entirely, and the phosphorescence of complex 1 at 560 nm could be evidently observed. Subsequently, we used TRES to detect the emission of the 1/G-quadruplex system with or without GLY with a decay time of 333 ns. The system showed a clear increase of emission signal when GLY was present, indicating that the TRES platform would be used to detect GLY (Fig. 3b).

To provide further confirmation of the mechanism of GLY detection by using complex 1, circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy and gel electrophoresis were performed. Compared with the GLY sequence only, CD spectroscopy of the GLY sequence incubated with 1 μM of GLY showed two higher maxima peaks at 287 and 270 nm and a weak RSC Advances negative signal at 245 nm, consistent with a hybrid-type G-quadruplex. (Fig. S2a). The alteration of the electrophoretic migration of the aptamer also indicated that GLY induced a conformational change in the GLY aptamer (Fig. S2b). These results are consistent with previous data [44] and support the hypothesis that the observed luminescence response of the system is most likely due to the formation of a hybrid-type G-quadruplex induced GLY.

To obtain better sensitivity, the concentration of complex 1, K+ and DNA, as well as pH, temperature, incubation time and denaturation time were separately optimized. The data showed a maximal signal was reached at 1 μM of complex 1 (Fig. 4 a). Meanwhile, the optimal K+ concentration was at 50 mM (Fig. 4b), the pH was optimal at 7.5 (Fig. 4c), the optimal denaturation temperature was 95 °C (Fig. 4d), the concentration of DNA was optimal at 80 nM (Fig. 4e), the optimal incubation time was 10 min (Fig. 4f) and the optional denaturation time was 5 min (Fig. 4g).

After optimization, the emission intensity of the 1/G-quadruplex platform with different amounts of GLY from 0.1 nM to 500 nM in Tris buffer (Fig. 5 a) or in soybean extract buffer (Fig. S3a) simulating a complex environment was recorded in TRES mode. The luminescence intensity increased with the concentration of GLY with linearity over 50–300 nM in Tris buffer (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) is 26.4 nM by using the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of background spectrum and S is the slope of the calibration curve. Encouragingly, the selectivity of the 1/G-quadruplex-based system still showed linearity between luminescence signal and GLY concentration from 100 to 500 nM in the presence of soybean extract, with a LOD of 79.3 nM (Fig. S3b).

To verify the selectivity of the assay for GLY, other herbicides and pesticides including methomyl, paraquat dichloride tetrahydrate (PDT), prometryne, and warfarin were tested for control studies. These compounds showed very low emission signal changes with the 1/G-quadruplex system by comparison with GLY both in Tris-Cl buffer (Fig. 6 a) and urine buffer simulating a complex environment (Fig. S4a). Moreover, we also observed no significant change of emission intensity with interfering ions such as Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, PO4 3− and CO3 2- in tris buffer (Fig. 6b) and urine buffer (Fig. S4b). The above results suggest that this method is highly selective for GLY, which we presume is based on the specific recognition properties of the GLY aptamer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GLY in complex environments
ce:section.ce:para: With high sensitivity and selectivity for GLY in aqueous solution, the 1/G-quadruplex platform was further tested in more complex samples. Due to the intense background fluorescence in biological samples caused by the presence of various proteins, vitamin, amino acids, and pigments, TRES was used for the detection [45,46]. We used the platform to detect GLY in plant extracts including licorice, ginseng, ginger and soybean, and in the extracts of LO2 and HUVEC cells. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery ratios of plant extracts ranged from 85 % to 110 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of under ±4.88 %. The recovery ratios in cell extracts were between 92 % and 102 % with a RSD of less than ±5.44 %. Taken together, these results suggest that the method has good accuracy and repeatability.

To examine the feasibility of the approach for testing for GLY in animal samples, mice were fed with GLY and the GLY content excreted in their urine after different time periods were determined using the 1/G-quadruplex system (Table 2 ). To further evaluate the application of the method, we have also detected TRES signals of GLY (200 nM), GLY + 1 (1 μM), 1 + aptamer (80 nM) and GLY + 1 + aptamer in mouse urine. We found that TRES mode can overcome the fluorescence interference of urine (Fig. S5). The TRES technique was validated by comparing paired samples with a commercial ELISA kit (Abraxis, USA). The correlation coefficients between TRES and ELISA were from 0.85 to 0.93, demonstrating the feasibility of the TRES approach for detecting GLY in animals exposed to GLY.

In conclusion, an iridium(III) complex/G-quadruplex-based platform for detecting GLY in complex samples was established. The iridium(III) complex 1 showed high affinity for G-quadruplex DNA over other kinds of DNA. Moreover, complex 1 exhibited a long-lived phosphorescence, allowing its emission to be observed even in a fluorescent environment via TRES. TRES is a technique that measures the emission at discrete times during the fluorescence decay process, which provides a potential route to overcome short-lived fluorescence background signals [17–19]. Under optimized conditions, the 1/G-quadruplex system could detect GLY with linearity from 50–300 nM in Tris buffer. The limit of detection (LOD) (3σ/S) by using this probe for GLY was 26.4 nM which is lower than other previously reported GLY detection methods (Table S2). The selectivity of the iridium(III) complex/G-quadruplex based system still showed linearity between emission intensity and GLY concentration from 100 to 500 nM in the presence of soybean extract, with an LOD of 79.3 nM. In plant samples, the recovery rates of GLY detected was between 85 % and 110 %, while in cell extracts the recovery rates were between 92 % and 102 %. Finally, the platform was able to detect GLY in the urine of mammals fed with GLY. This platform showed high correlation with ELISA, indicating the feasibility of this technique in detecting GLY in animals exposed to GLY. This platform provides an avenue for the rapid detection of GLY in complex samples, such as plant and clinical samples. Finally, besides being a rapid and efficient detection method, this method also has the potential for rapid diagnosis at the point of care. We anticipate the platform could be used in environmental monitoring and biological detection due to its sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity, and could also supplement traditional GLY detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
Feng Chen: Writing - original draft, Investigation. Guodong Li: Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Hao Liu: Investigation. Chung-Hang Leung: Supervision. Dik-Lung Ma: Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hong Kong Baptist University (FRG2/17-18/003), the Health and Medical Research Fund (HMRF/14150561), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575121 and 21775131), the Hong Kong Baptist University Century Club Sponsorship Scheme 2018, the Interdisciplinary Research Matching Scheme (RC-IRMS/16-17/03), Interdisciplinary Research Clusters Matching Scheme (RC-IRCs/17-18/03), Collaborative Research Fund (C5026-16G), SKLEBA and HKBU Strategic Development Fund (SKLP_1920_P02), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (File no. 0016/2020/A), and the University of Macau (MYRG2018-00187-ICMS and MYRG2019-00002-ICMS).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128393.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chem. Commun.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Feng Chen completed his Master of Science degree in Ningbo University, Zhejiang, China. He is currently undertaking his PhD studies at the University of Macau under the supervision of Dr. Chung-Hang Leung and Dr. Dik-Lung Ma. His research interest lies in the development of screening platforms for drug discovery and development.

Guodong Li completed his Master of Science degree in Medicinal Chemistry at Guangdong Medical University, Zhanjiang, China. He is currently undertaking his PhD studies at the University of Macau under the supervision of Dr. Chung-Hang Leung and Dr. Dik-Lung Ma. His research interest lies in the development of screening platforms for drug discovery and development.

Hao Liu completed his Master of Chemistry degree in Shanghai University, Shanghai, China. He is currently undertaking his PhD studies at the Hong Kong Baptist University under the supervision of Dr. Dik-Lung Ma and Dr. Chung-Hang Leung. His research interest lies in the development of screening platforms for detection probes and drug discovery development.

Chung-Hang Leung completed his PhD in 2002 at City University of Hong Kong. After completing a five year post-doctoral fellowship at the Department of Pharmacology, Yale University, he was appointed as a Research Assistant Professor at the University of Hong Kong and then at the Hong Kong Baptist University. He is currently appointed as a Professor at University of Macau. His primary research interests are in structure-based drug discovery and in the development of oligonucleotidebased drug screening methods.

Dik-Lung Ma completed his PhD in 2004 at the University of Hong Kong under the supervision of Prof. C.-M. Che. He spent the years 2005–2009 at the University of Hong Kong, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, and the Scripps Research Institute with Prof. C.-M. Che, Prof. K–Y. Wong, and Prof. R. Abagyan. He is currently an Associate Professor at the Hong Kong Baptist University. His research mainly focuses on luminescent sensing of biomolecules and metal ions, computer-aided drug discovery, and inorganic medicine.
